rulings.Case Title: BRYAN LOGAN VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, February 21, 2014, Line 2, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Granted. The Court did not consider the new evidence submitted in reply. Plaintiff did not establish a peculiar dangerous condition that would require Defendant to light the intersection. (Mixon v. PG&E (2012) 207 Cal.App.4th 124, 133.) Design immunity shields the City from any liability arising from the placement of the limit line or pedestrian signal pole. (Govt. Code. sec. 830.6.) Sign immunity shields the City from any liability arising from lack of signage at the intersection. (Govt. Code secs. 830.4 and 830.8.) Plaintiff has not established a triable issue of material fact as to whether the faded crosswalk line amounts to a dangerous condition, as defined in Govt. Code sec. 830. Although Plaintiff's expert speculates that the driver of the vehicle may have been unsure where to stop, the deposition testimony of the driver establishes that the driver was familiar with the intersection and knew where the crosswalk was located. (Declaration of David A. Delbon, Ex. C, Deposition of Nahil Sherzoy, 37:1-38:18, 40:10-17, 52:7-18.) Further, Plaintiff cannot demonstrate a substantial risk of injury when due care is used because a driver using due care should yield to pedestrians in an intersection even without a marked crosswalk pursuant to Veh. Code sec. 21950. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ehg

rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL PADWAY, INDIVIDUALLY AND DBA MICHAEL VS. NAPOLI, BERN,RIPKA & ASSOCIATES, LLC et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn For Judgment On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, February 21, 2014, Line 12, DEFENDANT NAPOLI BERN RIPKA & ASSOCIATES, LLP'S MOTION For Judgment On The Pleadings Motion for judgment on the pleadings is denied as to the first cause of action. Plaintiff alleges sufficient ultimate facts for the breach of contract cause of action. Motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted with 10 days leave to amend as to the second cause of action. Plaintiff is granted leave to plead with specificity the facts supporting each element of negligent misrepresentation, concealment, and/or false promise, particularly as to intent to defraud, intent to conceal, and lack of reasonable ground for believing representations were true, among other elements. rulings.Case Title: JASON BISHOP VS. CONSUMERTRACK INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, February 21, 2014, Line 18, DEFENDANT CONSUMERTRACK INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Overruled. (CCP § 430.10(e).) CAN-SPAM Act does not preempt Bus. & Prof. Code § 17529.5 ("§ 17529.5"). (Balsam v. Trancos, Inc. (2012) 203 Cal.App.4th 1083, 1102; Hypertouch, Inc. v. ValueClick, Inc. (2011) 192 Cal.App.4th 805, 817.) Plaintiff has pled sufficient facts to allege a violation of § 17529.5. For header information pursuant to § 17529.5(a)(2), Defendant concedes that Plaintiff has alleged that two emails sent by Defendant in which the "sender domain name . . . neither identifies the actual sender on its face nor is readily traceable to the sender using a publicly available online database such as WHOIS." (Balsam, supra, 203 Cal.App.4th at 1101. See MPA, p. 7:14-15.) Plaintiff need not allege a misrepresentation, reliance or damages. (Hypertouch, Inc., supra, 192 Cal.App.4th at 820, 821, 822.) For subject line information pursuant to § 17529(a)(3), Defendant's demurrer does not address the allegations pertaining to this violation. To the extent that Defendant specially demurs for uncertainty, these grounds are not stated in the demurrer. (MPA, p. 10:4-18; Demurrer, p. 2:7-22.) The proffering of supporting and opposing declarations by both parties is improper and not considered by the Court. Plaintiff's unopposed request for judicial notice is granted to the extent that the pleading was filed, but not to the facts asserted therein. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/eg
